The work you're going to see Tony doing is a little more subtle and sophisticated than you've seen in many of the training films. The first session is usually a round table session. So the round table session is where Tony sits down and meets those people for the first time around a round table and he interviews them in depth in order to understand them. And you'll see him asking more in depth questions and listening more than you've seen in any of the videos that are based on events, on stage. So systemically speaking, in any complex system that's operating in the world there's usually just one thing that's really holding it back. It's one bottleneck, one holdback. It's holding back the whole system. So this insight actually comes from managing factories. Now if you were trying to make, if you're a factory person and you're trying to make a factory more efficient like a production line, there's only one part of that line that's slowing down the whole process. So that's the part that needs your attention. Anything else you do might help the factory but it won't speed things up. To give you another metaphor that might be a little closer to home, if your car isn't working out of all your car systems there's only one system that you need to fix first. So if a car is out of gas then changing the oil won't hurt. It won't help changing the spark close won't help putting air in the tire won't help. It won't start the car. So there's only thing that will help is putting the gas in the car and so that's a holdback. Do you want to add anything about Erickson right now, Chloe? Oh, okay. I went to see him when my father died and I couldn't get over the grief. And so I called him on somebody's recommendation. I was quite young then and I had two little girls. One of them was my age. And he said, you have to come and stay here for a week in a hotel and I'm going to see you every day. And I said, I have two little girls. I can't go there for a week. And he said, bring them. So I brought them. So my Ali actually played with Erickson. And so I brought them with an Annie and I had to go to Erickson's house early in the morning, like around nine. He would talk to me for a while. Then let me take a break at the hotel or most of the time, had me sitting in his living room because his office was in his home. So he didn't really have a waiting room. It was the living room. And then would see somebody else while I waited and then would talk to me again. And from the very beginning, he antagonized me completely. She told me that I really hadn't had a father. Then my father was not very fatherly. And I thought, I don't understand anything. And he got me so angry that I completely came out of my depression. I went to the other side of the crazy rage, the age, and I was enraged. And then he could work with that better. I was better when I was enraged than when I was depressed. I was more intelligent, I guess. And then as we continued talking, he would send me to the living room and he insisted that I had to read the biography of Margaret Mead. And I would say to him, why do I have to read this? And he said, someday you'll understand. And he totally focused me on my career, completely encouraging my career. And with that focus, I was able to let go of my personal problems and my grief and so on. And I remember the last thing that I said is I've been so depressed now for a couple of months. I'm worried about my little girls. And he said, bring them. And so my two daughters came to the house and played in the yard with Erickson and his dogs. And he said to me, they're just fine. And you know what you have to do. And I wasn't sure what I had to do. But I guess that the idea of the comparison with Margaret Mead was that I was going to have a sort of up and down life, but that I was going to be able to make a contribution like she did. And he was a very good friend of hers. So that's amazing. So that's a really powerful use of a strategy. And he was a strategic interventionist in the broader sense and he believed in distributing these tools to more people and not just one person. He wanted the doctors and the dentists to have this. So one of the things we see in a lot of the breakthrough sessions, the challenges that Tony gives, they come from a practice of ordeal coaching or ordeal therapy is another word for it. And Chloe, this is a story of Jay's. So Jay was really my father most of my life. And he was also a founder of family therapy. And I was a photographer for a long time. And he used to tell me a story I loved. And I've always remembered about a photographer who was a client who had the problem that even though he was very successful, he kept making mistakes on his photo shoots. And just he knew he would unconsciously destroy his whole business with these stupid errors he would make. And he was really anxious and it was destroying his professional life, his personal life. He wasn't able to sleep. It was really messing him up. So the ordeal that was given to him as a strategy to use was he had to go out on his next assignment and not put any film in the camera at all. This was, of course, before digital. So imagine going on a very important client shoot, thousands of dollars, your reputation on the line, and you would consciously not load your camera with any film the whole day. Completely on purpose, ruin your shoot, your reputation, everything. So the coach in this case got the client to commit to making this mistake on purpose. And the result being that after doing this type of challenge, that client, that photographer, never made another error. And this can work with insomnia. That's a great story, and I do remember it. I'm also one of my favorites. Sometimes that one's powerful because it is custom tailored to his exact challenge. And that's usually how it is.
